speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 46: How Git Works: The Mental Model. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, How Git Works: The Mental Model should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Commits, branches, staging, local versus remote, push, pull, fetch, and why conflicts happen. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is understanding first, then action, then confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We are building a rhythm: orient yourself, take one intentional action, then verify what changed before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: 1. Why a Mental Model Matters: On Day 1, you edited files on GitHub.com using the web editor. The next useful detail is concrete: GitHub handled Git for you behind the scenes -- creating commits, managing branches, and tracking changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. Git organizes your work into three areas. Put another way, understanding these three areas is the single most important concept in this chapter. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Working directory: the working directory is the folder on your computer where the files live. That matters in practice: When you open a project in VS Code, everything you see in the file explorer is the working directory.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Staging area (also called the index). The staging area is a holding zone. This is the part to say slowly: When you are happy with a change in the working directory, you add it to the staging area.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: the repository is Git's permanent record. The listener should be able to check this: When you commit, Git takes everything in the staging area and stores it as a snapshot -- a permanent record of what those files looked like at that moment.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat How the three areas connect as decoration. Many visual Git tutorials use diagrams with arrows to show this flow. That is not trivia. The text description above and the three-step sequence are the same information without requiring a visual representation. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, edit files in the working directory. Then, stage the changes you want to keep (add to the staging area). After that, commit the staged changes (save to the repository). That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, An analogy: packing a box is still useful because think of it like packing a box to mail. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: You can put items in and take them out of the box (stage and unstage) as many times as you want before sealing it (committing).

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. The working directory is your desk with papers and items scattered on it. The staging area is the open box on the floor -- you put items into it as you decide what to ship. The commit is sealing the box, labeling it, and putting it on the shelf -- once sealed, its contents are recorded permanently.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Learning Cards: The Three Areas has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Run git status in the terminal (Ctrl+\`) -- it announces which files are in each area (working directory, staging, committed) with clear labels. In VS Code Source Control (Ctrl+Shift+G), files are grouped under "Changes" (working directory) and "Staged Changes" (staging area) -- navigate with arrow keys. Press Enter on any file in the Source Control panel to hear the diff -- added and removed lines are announced with change-type prefixes. In Source Control (Ctrl+Shift+G), staged files appear under a separate "Staged Changes" heading with a green + icon; unstaged files are under "Changes" with an orange M icon. Use Ctrl+= to increase editor font size if the Source Control file list is hard to read at default zoom. The gutter indicator (colored bar on the left edge of the editor) shows green for added lines and blue for modified lines.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. A commit is a snapshot of your project at a specific moment in time. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: It is not a diff (a list of changes).

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because every commit gets a unique identifier -- a 40-character string called a SHA hash. That gives the learner a foothold: in practice, you usually see only the first 7 characters: a1b2c3d. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Commits are permanent (mostly): Once a commit is made, it is part of the repository's history. The next useful detail is concrete: You can make new commits that undo the changes, but the original commit still exists in the timeline.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Learning Cards: Commits has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. After committing, run git log --oneline -5 in the terminal to hear the last 5 commit hashes and messages read aloud. In VS Code, press Ctrl+Shift+G then navigate to the commit message input box -- your screen reader announces "Message" -- type your description and press Ctrl+Enter to commit. The 7-character short hash (e.g., a1b2c3d) is what Git commands accept -- you do not need the full 40 characters. The Source Control commit input box is at the top of the Source Control panel -- increase panel width by dragging the panel edge if the text is truncated. After committing, the file count badge on the Source Control icon drops to zero, confirming the commit succeeded. Use Timeline view (Ctrl+Shift+P then "Focus on Timeline View") to see a chronological list of commits for the current file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 4. What Is a Branch? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 4. What Is a Branch?: see also: Chapter 14: Git in Practice shows how to create and switch branches hands-on in VS Code. That matters in practice: A branch is a name that points to a specific commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on The default branch: main. Every repository has a default branch, usually called main. This is the part to say slowly: When you clone a repository, Git checks out the main branch, which means it loads the files from the commit that main points to into your working directory. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: when you create a new branch, Git creates a new pointer that starts at the same commit you are currently on. The listener should be able to check this: Both branches point to the same commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Making commits on a branch as decoration. When you make a new commit while on the fix/typo branch, the fix/typo pointer moves forward to the new commit. That is not trivia. The main pointer stays where it was.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on HEAD: which branch are you on? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, HEAD: which branch are you on? is still useful because Git uses a special pointer called HEAD to track which branch you are currently working on. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: When you switch branches (checkout), Git moves HEAD to point to the new branch and updates the files in your working directory to match.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Learning Cards: Branches has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. Press Ctrl+Shift+G then Tab to reach the branch name in the Source Control panel -- your screen reader announces the current branch. The Status Bar at the bottom of VS Code shows the current branch name -- navigate to it with F6 to cycle through Status Bar items. To switch branches, press Ctrl+Shift+P then type "Git: Checkout to" and arrow through the branch list. The current branch name appears in the bottom-left of the Status Bar -- click it to see a dropdown of all branches. Branch names in the Status Bar use the same font size as the rest of the bar; zoom the entire window with Ctrl+= if it is too small. In the Source Control panel, the branch name is shown above the commit input -- verify it before committing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 5. Local vs Remote. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. See also: Appendix D: Git Authentication covers how to set up credentials so push and pull work without password prompts. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: So far, we have talked about one repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because the remote repository is the one on GitHub.com. That gives the learner a foothold: when you see a repository URL like github.com/Community-Access/git-going-with-github, that is the remote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The local repository. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The local repository: The local repository is the copy on your computer. The next useful detail is concrete: When you run git clone, Git downloads the entire repository -- all files, all branches, all history -- to your machine.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. The text diagram above shows two boxes side by side connected by arrows. Put another way, the left box is labeled "Your computer (local)" and contains working directory, staging area, and repository. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Local vs Remote. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Local vs Remote has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Run git remote -v in the terminal to hear which remote URLs are configured -- typically origin pointing to your GitHub repository. After git push, your screen reader announces the output including the branch name and commit count sent -- listen for "Everything up-to-date" if nothing new to push. Run git status to hear whether your local branch is ahead of, behind, or in sync with the remote tracking branch. The Status Bar sync indicator (bottom-left, next to the branch name) shows up/down arrow counts: up-arrows = commits to push, down-arrows = commits to pull. Click the sync icon in the Status Bar to push and pull in one action -- the arrows disappear when local and remote are in sync. The Source Control panel heading shows the repository name and branch so you can confirm which remote you are working.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on 6. Push, Pull, and Fetch. These three operations keep your local and remote repositories synchronized.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: git push sends your local commits to the remote. The listener should be able to check this: After pushing, anyone who looks at the repository on GitHub.com will see your changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git push origin fix/typo. git push -u origin fix/typo. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Pull: get other people's work as decoration. git pull downloads new commits from the remote and immediately merges them into your current branch. That is not trivia. This is how you get changes that other people (or you on another computer) have pushed. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git pull origin main. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Fetch: check for updates without merging is still useful because git fetch downloads new commits from the remote but does not change your working directory or current branch. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It just updates your local knowledge of what the remote looks like.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git fetch origin. git log main.origin/main --oneline. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 7. Why Merge Conflicts Happen into plain language. A merge conflict happens when Git cannot automatically combine two sets of changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When conflicts occur. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Conflicts happen when two branches modify the same lines in the same file. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Git knows how to merge changes to different files, and even different parts of the same file.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. This part earns its place because when a conflict occurs, Git marks the conflicting section in the file with special markers. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to resolve a conflict: The Day 1 connection: In Chapter 7, you resolved a merge conflict on GitHub.com using the web editor. The next useful detail is concrete: On Day 2, you will resolve conflicts in VS Code, where the editor highlights the markers and offers buttons to accept one side or the other.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open the file with the conflict markers. Then, read both versions and decide which text to keep (or write a new version that combines both). After that, delete the conflict markers ( ). Finally, save the file. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Stage and commit the resolved file. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. They happen in every project where more than one person works at the same time. Put another way, the fact that Git stops and asks is a safety feature -- it prevents data loss by never silently choosing one version over another.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Merge Conflicts. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Merge Conflicts has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. When a conflict occurs, VS Code announces "Merge conflict" and the file appears in Source Control under "Merge Changes" -- navigate with arrow keys. Use F7 in the diff viewer to step through conflict hunks; each hunk announces the line range and both versions of the conflicting text. After resolving, stage the file (Ctrl+Shift+G, navigate to the file, press +) then commit to complete the merge. Conflict markers ( ) appear as highlighted blocks in the editor -- look for colored backgrounds (green for current, blue for incoming). VS Code places "Accept Current Change", "Accept Incoming Change", and "Accept Both" buttons inline above each conflict -- they are large enough to click at high zoom. Increase editor zoom with Ctrl+= to make the conflict marker labels easier to read.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on 8. The Git Timeline. Every commit has a parent pointer (except the very first commit). This is the part to say slowly: This creates a chain -- a timeline of the project's history. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Reading the timeline. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: the timeline reads backward: the most recent commit points to the one before it, which points to the one before that, all the way back to the first commit. The listener should be able to check this: When you run git log, Git follows these pointers from the current commit backward and shows you each commit's message, author, date, and hash.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat Branching creates parallel timelines as decoration. When two branches diverge (both have commits that the other does not), the timeline splits into two parallel paths. That is not trivia. Both branches share commits A and B (their common history).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Merging reconnects timelines. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Merging reconnects timelines is still useful because when you merge fix/typo into main, Git creates a new merge commit that has two parents: the latest commit on main and the latest commit on fix/typo. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: The two timelines join back together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Put 9. Putting It All Together into plain language. Here is the complete workflow that you will practice in Chapter 14, translated through the mental model. The useful version is: Everything in this table maps directly to the three areas (Section 2), the two copies (Section 5), and the timeline (Section 8). The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Quick mental model checklist. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Before running a Git command, ask yourself. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: If you can answer these four questions, you can troubleshoot almost any Git situation.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Where am I? Which branch is HEAD on? (git status tells you). What has changed? Are there modifications in the working directory? Staged changes? (git status tells you). Which direction? Am I pushing (local to remote) or pulling (remote to local)? What could conflict? Has anyone else changed the same files on the same branch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because next: Chapter 14: Git in Practice Back: Chapter 12: VS Code Accessibility Related appendices: Appendix E: Advanced Git Appendix D: Git Authentication.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 46. Next in the series is episode 47, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
